text: In recent years, China has seen a wave of successful and largely peaceful protests against para-xylene (PX) plants in cities such as Xiamen, Dalian, and Ningbo. The 2014 Maoming protest, however, stands as a significant and violent departure from this trend. Drawing on participant observation and in-depth interviews, this article offers several key insights into this anomaly. First, despite aggressive government propaganda, residents of Maoming possessed a sophisticated understanding of the environmental risks associated with PX production, informed by both their lived experience with local pollution and awareness of the earlier Xiamen incident. Second, the Maoming protest lacked a formal organizational structure, a factor that contributed to its escalation into violence. Finally, the study demonstrates how the movement achieved a massive scale without the backing of mainstream media or the urban middle class, relying instead on the strategic use of social media and a deep, localized knowledge of the city’s physical terrain.
